title: Pinch-pot egg cup set
subtitle: Two small egg cups pinched from balls of clay and finished with a small foot
excerpt: Two small pinch-pot egg cups, formed from balls of air-dry clay and finished with a foot ring pressed onto the base. The cups are decorative; air-dry clay is not waterproof or food-safe, so they sit alongside the breakfast egg as ornaments rather than holding a soft-boiled egg directly.
sourceNotes: Pinch-pot forming is the oldest documented ceramic technique. V&A open-access collection for historical egg-cup form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A vessel formed by pressing the thumb into a ball of clay and walking the wall around between thumb and finger.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: sized to cradle the bottom of a boiled egg, with a short pressed foot to keep it stable on the saucer. Two cups make a matched pair for two settings. No kiln required. The cups use air-dry clay; they are decorative pieces, sitting next to the egg on a plate rather than holding the egg directly, because air-dry clay is not food-safe even sealed.

items.name: An egg as a sizing reference

content.text: Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board. Divide into two equal balls of 75 g each. A 75 g ball rolls smooth between the palms in about 30 seconds. Check the surface for cracks; if there are any, work a few drops of water in and re-wedge.
Each cup is built the same way. Start with one ball; the second follows after the first is on the drying board.

Hold the ball cupped in the left hand. Press the right thumb into the centre from the top, stopping 6 mm from the bottom. This forms the base of the cup. Pressing through to the table makes the base too thin to hold the walls upright as they stiffen.

Pinch the wall between thumb (inside) and index finger (outside). Rotate the ball a quarter-turn between pinches. Work from the base upward in slow passes rather than running straight to the rim. Aim for a 5 mm wall thickness throughout. The opening should be about 38 mm across, just wide enough to take the wider end of an egg.

Set an egg lightly into the cup, wider end down. The egg should sit with about 25 mm of its length below the rim and the rest above. If the opening is too tight, gently widen the rim with a thumb. If too loose, pinch the rim inward a few mm. Lift the egg out carefully.
Run the wooden rib in slow circular strokes around the inside wall to smooth the pinch marks. Trim any high points on the rim with the needle tool, then sponge once around the edge to round it.

Roll a small coil about 5 mm thick and 90 mm long from a pinch of clay. Score the base of the cup with the needle tool and brush with thin slurry. Press the coil onto the base in a circle, leaving 6 mm of clear base inside the ring. Press the cut ends together and smooth the ring into the base from inside the cup with a fingertip. The foot raises the cup 5 mm off the table and adds a small visual lift.

Repeat the same steps with the second 75 g ball. Aim to match the first: the same rim diameter, the same wall thickness, the same foot height. A matched pair reads as a set; mismatched cups look unfinished.

Set both cups upright on the board. Cover loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first 12 hours: a wet rim and dry base crack apart as they shrink at different rates. Remove the plastic once the cups reach
and let them finish drying open. Full drying to

Once bone-dry, sand the rim, the foot, and the inside of the cup with 220-grit sandpaper. Paint with acrylics or leave plain. Seal inside and out with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish, paying attention to the inside surface where moisture from a cool egg would land.

Air-dry clay is not food-safe; the cups are decorative. Use them alongside the egg on a plate rather than holding a hot soft-boiled egg directly. Wipe with a damp cloth. Keep indoors and away from a wet sink.
Press a small repeating pattern around the outside wall while the clay is still soft using the back of a fork or a carved stamp.
Make a set of four in the same way; the wedging and pinching pattern repeats with no extra equipment.
Build a matching small salt cellar in the same form, opening to a 50 mm rim and leaving the foot off for a flatter shape.

items.symptom: Cup tips over once the foot is attached
items.cause: Foot ring uneven in height around the circle, or coil pressed too thin on one side
items.fix: Press the cup gently down onto the board while the clay is still leather-hard to level the foot; trim any high points with the fettling knife

items.cause: Walls pinched too thin, or rim dried faster than the base
items.fix: Aim for 5 mm walls; cover with plastic for the first 12 hours; press fresh slurry into any small cracks before they widen

items.symptom: Egg slips down too far into the cup
items.cause: Rim opening was widened too much during forming
items.fix: Pinch the rim back in a few mm while the clay is still soft, or build a thicker foot to raise the cup so less egg sits inside
